questions.question: Explain the event-driven programming model and its advantages over procedural programming.
keyPoints: Event-driven programming responds to user actions/events rather than sequential execution
Provides better interactivity and responsiveness in GUI applications
Allows non-blocking operations and parallel processing capabilities
Improves modularity through event handlers and delegates

questions.question: Describe the event propagation mechanism with reference to capture and bubble phases.
keyPoints: W3C model defines three phases: Capture, Target, and Bubble
Capture phase: event flows from root to target element
Target phase: event reaches the target element
Bubble phase: event bubbles up from target to root
Developers can register handlers at any phase using addEventListener

questions.question: What is event delegation? Explain its advantages with an example.
keyPoints: Event delegation attaches single listener to parent element
Handles events from multiple child elements using bubbling
Reduces memory consumption significantly
Useful for dynamic elements added dynamically
Example: Single click handler on <ul> can handle clicks on all <li> children

questions.question: Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous events in event-driven programming.
keyPoints: Synchronous events: occur and processed immediately in sequence, blocking execution
Asynchronous events: occur unpredictably, handled via callbacks without blocking
Event loop handles both types in different ways
Asynchronous model keeps UI responsive during long operations
Examples: setTimeout (async), file read with callback (async)

questions.question: Explain the role of the event loop and event queue in handling user inputs.
keyPoints: Event loop continuously monitors for events in the queue
Event queue stores events in order of occurrence (FIFO)
Event loop dequeues events and dispatches to appropriate handlers
Allows sequential processing of asynchronous events
Prevents loss of events during high-frequency input

questions.question: What are delegates in C#? How do they facilitate event-driven programming?
keyPoints: Delegate is a type-safe function pointer in C#
Holds reference to methods with specific signature
Allows dynamic method invocation at runtime
Events in C# are built using delegates
Publisher defines delegate, subscriber provides handler method

questions.question: List and explain four common types of GUI events with examples.
keyPoints: Mouse events: click, double-click, drag, hover
Keyboard events: keydown, keyup, keypress
Window events: resize, minimize, close, load
Timer events: interval elapse, timeout
Focus events: focus, blur, change

questions.question: Explain the complete event handling process in a GUI application. Include event generation, queuing, dispatching, and execution phases with a diagram.
keyPoints: User interaction generates event at GUI component
Event is created with relevant data (type, target, coordinates)
Event is placed in event queue in order of occurrence
Event loop continuously monitors queue and dequeues events
Dispatcher identifies appropriate handler based on event type and source
Handler method is invoked with event object as parameter
Handler executes and may update UI or trigger further events
Process repeats for next event in queue
Diagram showing: User → Component → Event → Queue → Loop → Dispatcher → Handler → Response

questions.question: Design an event-driven system for a simple calculator with buttons 0-9, +, -, *, /, =, and C (clear). Explain the event handlers required and how they would be implemented.
keyPoints: Define Calculator class with display and button components
Register click handlers for each button type
Number button handlers: append digit to display expression
Operator button handlers: store current value and operator
Equals handler: evaluate expression and display result
Clear handler: reset all values and display
Event delegation can be used for number buttons
State management using variables for operand1, operand2, operator
Error handling for division by zero and invalid operations
Code demonstration with pseudocode or language-specific implementation

questions.question: Explain the Observer pattern and its relevance to event-driven programming. How is it implemented in popular frameworks?
keyPoints: Observer pattern defines one-to-many dependency between objects
Subject/Observable maintains list of observers
When subject state changes, it notifies all observers
In event-driven terms: Event Source is Subject, Listeners are Observers
Decouples event producers from consumers
Implementation in Java: EventListenerList, PropertyChangeSupport
Implementation in C#: Events and Delegates based on Observer
Implementation in JavaScript: addEventListener follows this pattern
Benefits: loose coupling, dynamic relationships, scalability
Real-world examples: Button click (subject) with multiple listeners

questions.question: Compare event handling mechanisms in at least three different programming languages/frameworks. Include syntax and practical examples.
keyPoints: JavaScript: addEventListener(element, 'click', handler) with event object
Java: Component.addActionListener(new ActionListener { method })
C#: event with delegate, += operator to subscribe
Python Tkinter: command=handler or bind method
Java uses interfaces for type-safe event handling
C# uses delegates and lambda expressions
JavaScript supports both traditional and modern async/await patterns
Differences in event propagation models across languages
Similarities: all use callback pattern, event objects carry data
